caveats: a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and is reported at the broader NAICS 238100 ancestor.
a: Occupation shares are employment-weighted, while this primitive is wage- and task-weighted.
a: The estimate concerns technically exposed current tasks, not realized adoption.
rho: Survey expectations are not measured implementation outcomes.
rho: The survey spans construction segments and firm sizes rather than the target lens.
rho: Robotics and AI are combined in the source, while the primitive concerns the exposed opportunity identified here.
e: No source directly measures eligibility among firms in the supplied EBITDA band.
e: Licensing, subcontracting norms, and owner dependence vary substantially by state and end market.
e: The supplied firm count is an estimate and uses ancestor-level inputs.
s5: The Census age distribution is for responding owners across employer industries and dates to reference year 2018.
s5: BizBuySell transactions are voluntarily reported and cover broad building and construction businesses, not framing alone.
s5: The range excludes closures, deaths without transferable operations, minority investments, and internal reorganizations.
q: The source is an industry software vendor rather than a representative government survey.
q: The cited 80% figure covers construction projects across trades and vintages, not framing contractors specifically.
q: Actual retention depends on local bid density, builder concentration, contract duration, and whether savings are observable.
d5: Occupational employment is an imperfect demand proxy and includes self-employment and carpentry outside NAICS 238130.
d5: The BLS projection is ten-year and the conversion to five years assumes a smooth path.
d5: Construction demand is highly cyclical and geographically uneven.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for framing contractors.
o: Offsite prefabrication adoption could move faster in repetitive multifamily and single-family production than in custom or alteration work.
o: This primitive addresses who must deliver the service, not task automation already reflected in a and rho.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based subcontract revenue, commonly bid as a fixed-price or lump-sum scope and billed by percentage of completion; some work uses time-and-materials or unit-price terms. Repeat business comes from recurring builder and general-contractor relationships rather than subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Structural-steel or concrete framing, offsite component manufacturing, captive framing crews, labor-only shells without transferable customer relationships or supervision, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market contractors performing structural framing and sheathing, including wood, light-gauge steel, post framing, and onsite truss or wood-frame component work for builders, general contractors, developers, and property owners.

a.bridge: May 2023 OEWS measures the broader NAICS 238100 ancestor, not framing contractors alone. It shows 71.91% of employment in construction/extraction occupations, while office/administrative support is 8.30%, business/financial operations 4.35%, and management 5.85%; the estimate applies high task exposure to portions of estimating, project management, bookkeeping, bidding, scheduling, and document handling, and low exposure to physical framing. Framing likely has more carpenters and fewer noncraft roles than the ancestor, so the ancestor mix is more likely to overstate exposure.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and is reported at the broader NAICS 238100 ancestor.
Occupation shares are employment-weighted, while this primitive is wage- and task-weighted.
The estimate concerns technically exposed current tasks, not realized adoption.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study across at least 30 framing contractors, mapping hours and wages to estimating, takeoff, scheduling, project documentation, supervision, layout, cutting, and installation, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Most wage cost is attached to measuring, cutting, fastening, lifting, layout, supervision, and jobsite adjustment that current AI cannot substitute. The exposed pool is therefore chiefly office workflow plus limited planning, takeoff, documentation, and coordination time embedded in supervisory roles.

d5.bridge: BLS projects carpenter employment to rise from 959,000 in 2024 to 1,002,100 in 2034, a 4% ten-year increase, and attributes support to new-home and factory construction while identifying modular and prefabricated components as an offset. Applying roughly half of that ten-year employment change gives a five-year central anchor near 1.02. Employment is not constant-price, constant-quality service demand: productivity and trade substitution can separate them, and carpenters span industries beyond framing, so the range is widened in both directions.
caveats: Occupational employment is an imperfect demand proxy and includes self-employment and carpentry outside NAICS 238130.
The BLS projection is ten-year and the conversion to five years assumes a smooth path.
Construction demand is highly cyclical and geographically uneven.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar forecast of U.S. structural framing square footage by residential, multifamily, and nonresidential end market, combined with framing-material shares and repair/addition volumes, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Population-linked building needs and nonresidential construction support framing volume, while housing cyclicality, interest rates, factory-built components, and material substitution create substantial downside and upside around a nearly flat-to-modestly-growing central case.

caveats: No source directly measures eligibility among firms in the supplied EBITDA band.
Licensing, subcontracting norms, and owner dependence vary substantially by state and end market.
The supplied firm count is an estimate and uses ancestor-level inputs.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of state contractor-license rosters, payroll records, and 100 confidential information memoranda for framing businesses in the supplied earnings band could measure captive status, customer concentration, owner dependence, and transferable operating infrastructure.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is predominantly external project work and includes new work, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs. Most firms in the supplied earnings band should therefore be operating contractors eligible under the lens, but the estimate removes captive crews, labor-only shells, firms whose economics depend entirely on a departing owner, and businesses without transferable customer or supervisory infrastructure.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23813 ($5,011,841,752) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23813 ($26,247,424,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23813. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $295,409 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238130); class <5: 8,907 firms, avg revenue/firm $487,097 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $38,773 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,010 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,719,093 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $136,840 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 860 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,970,187 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $316,027 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 584 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,227,046 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,052,873 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $54,996,583 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,377,728 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $113,047,222 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,998,559 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 698

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for framing contractors.
Offsite prefabrication adoption could move faster in repetitive multifamily and single-family production than in custom or alteration work.
This primitive addresses who must deliver the service, not task automation already reflected in a and rho.
o.change_evidence: Builder procurement data measuring framing square footage delivered by independent subcontractors versus self-performed crews, panelized/modular suppliers, and robotic systems, segmented by end market over five years, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Framing requires accountable onsite execution: following plans, measuring and cutting materials, constructing walls and floors, directing helpers, adapting to field conditions, and checking completed work. Prefabricated walls, roofs, and other components can reduce onsite carpenter hours and shift quantity to manufacturers or builder self-performance, but remaining assembly, layout, safety, sequencing, inspection response, and warranty responsibility still require an operator.

q.bridge: A construction subcontractor billing provider reports that fixed-price work is the most common contract form between subcontractors and general contractors, cites 80% fixed-price usage among 38,000 projects entered in another construction-software dataset during 2018-2020, and explains that subcontractors can keep gains when labor hours come in below estimate. That supports near-term retention within a signed project, but it does not measure five-year discounted pass-through for framing. The transformation assumes short project cycles and competitive rebidding pass much of a visible productivity gain to customers over subsequent bids. Software-user projects may overrepresent organized residential builders, so fixed-price prevalence may be higher than in the full lens.
caveats: The source is an industry software vendor rather than a representative government survey.
The cited 80% figure covers construction projects across trades and vintages, not framing contractors specifically.
Actual retention depends on local bid density, builder concentration, contract duration, and whether savings are observable.
q.change_evidence: Matched bid, cost, and renewal data from at least 20 framing subcontractors before and after workflow automation, including contract type, win rate, gross margin, change orders, and customer repricing by cohort, would materially move this estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed-price contracts let the operator retain realized labor savings on work already awarded, but framing scopes rebid frequently and general contractors can compare bids. Customer bargaining, estimator learning, and competitor adoption therefore erode retention over five years, while execution risk and rework remain with the contractor.

rho.bridge: The 2025 AGC-NCCER survey covers construction firms broadly, not framing contractors or the exposed task pool. It reports 45% expecting robotics and AI to automate manual or error-prone tasks over five years and 44% expecting safer or more productive jobs; it also reports that fewer than half of firms with revenue of $50 million or less adopted certain modern recruiting approaches. These adoption attitudes anchor the middle of the range, while smaller-firm process fragmentation and jobsite variability pull expected implementation below full technical exposure. The broad survey may overstate implementation for small framing contractors because respondents include larger and more technology-capable firms.
caveats: Survey expectations are not measured implementation outcomes.
The survey spans construction segments and firm sizes rather than the target lens.
Robotics and AI are combined in the source, while the primitive concerns the exposed opportunity identified here.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor telemetry or an independent longitudinal survey of framing contractors under $50 million revenue showing task-level AI use, hours displaced, error rates, and retention after 12 months would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Off-the-shelf estimating, document extraction, scheduling, billing, and project-communication tools can reach part of the exposed opportunity within five years. Data cleanliness, integration with plans and field conditions, safety accountability, owner dependence, and limited change capacity prevent complete implementation.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses were at least 55 in 2018, while BizBuySell reports 3,142 sold building-and-construction listings analyzed and annual transaction observations from 2021 through 2025. Neither source supplies a framing-specific eligible-firm denominator or isolates control transfers. The estimate translates broad owner-age pressure and demonstrated construction deal flow into a five-year probability, then discounts for businesses that close, transfer internally, or cannot support a qualifying sale. The generic age statistic may overstate framing transfers if younger owners predominate; broker-reported deal data may understate private transfers but overrepresent small deals below the lens.
caveats: The Census age distribution is for responding owners across employer industries and dates to reference year 2018.
BizBuySell transactions are voluntarily reported and cover broad building and construction businesses, not framing alone.
The range excludes closures, deaths without transferable operations, minority investments, and internal reorganizations.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort from contractor-license ownership changes, PitchBook/Capital IQ control transactions, SBA 7(a) acquisition loans, and broker closed-deal files filtered to NAICS 238130 and the supplied earnings band would materially change this range.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership and visible construction-business transactions support meaningful transfer probability, but retirement intent is not a sale and many small trade contractors are too owner-dependent to transfer as operating companies.

principal_driver: AI can compress scarce estimating, project-control, and back-office work while fixed-price projects preserve some near-term savings.
principal_weakness: Most compensation funds physical onsite craft work, and competitive project rebidding rapidly exposes productivity gains to customers.

sources.supports: Defines framing contractors as establishments primarily engaged in structural framing and sheathing other than structural steel or concrete, including new work, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS: 238130 Framing Contractors

sources.supports: Reports 978,830 jobs in the broader industry, including occupation shares of 71.91% construction/extraction, 8.30% office/administrative support, 4.35% business/financial operations, and 5.85% management; also reports cost estimators, project-management specialists, construction managers, supervisors, carpenters, and laborers.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 238100

sources.supports: Describes carpenter duties and physical work, reports 959,000 jobs in 2024 and projected 2034 employment of 1,002,100, and says population and factory construction support demand while modular and prefabricated components reduce the need for onsite carpentry.
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Carpenters

sources.supports: AGC-NCCER construction survey reports 45% expect robotics and AI to automate manual or error-prone tasks over five years, 44% expect safer or more productive jobs, and smaller firms lag some modernization approaches; it also documents widespread craft and salaried hiring difficulty.
sources.title: 2025 Workforce Survey Analysis

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey, using 2018 data, with stated population limitations.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Reports 3,142 sold building-and-construction listings analyzed, annual transaction pricing from 2021 through 2025, and explains that the sample is based on construction businesses sold on and reported to BizBuySell.
sources.title: Construction Business Valuation Benchmarks

sources.supports: Explains fixed-price, time-and-materials, cost-plus, and unit-price subcontract billing; says fixed price is most common between subcontractors and GCs, cites 80% fixed-price usage among 38,000 software-recorded projects, and notes contractors can retain gains from lower labor hours or material cost on fixed-price work.
sources.title: 4 Main Types of Construction Contracts and How to Bill for Each

title: Framing Contractors
